heading: 5.5.1.1.Sub-byte Types
content:
##### 5.5.1.1.1. [Padding and alignment of the sub-byte types](#tensor-dimension-size-format-sub-bytes-padding-align)

The sub-byte types are expected to packed contiguously in the global memory and
the Tensor copy instruction will expand them by appending empty spaces as shown below:

1. Type `.b4x16`:
   With this type, there is no padding involved and the packed sixteen `.b4` elements
   in a 64-bits container is copied as is between the shared memory and the global memory.
2. Type `.b4x16_p64`:
   With this type, sixteen contiguous 4-bits of data is copied from global memory to the
   shared memory with the append of 64-bits of padding as shown in
   [Figure 5](#tensor-dimension-size-format-sub-bytes-padding-align-b4-16-p64)

   



[Figure: tensor-dimension-size-format-sub-bytes-padding-align-b4-16-p64]
LAYOUT FOR .b4x16_p64

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout transformation for the .b4x16_p64 format, showing how data is transferred from Global Memory to Shared Memory using the cp.async.bulk.tensor instruction. The layout involves a padding scheme where each 8-byte data chunk is followed by an 8-byte padding region in shared memory.

SECTION 1: SOURCE FORMAT (.b4x16 in Global Memory)

At the top of the figure, a wide horizontal bar is labeled ".b4x16" with "LSB" on the left end and "MSB" on the right end (with an arrow pointing right to MSB). This bar represents the .b4x16 format as a densely packed sequence of small colored cells (4-bit elements, 16 per group, repeating). The colors cycle through a repeating pattern: green, light blue, orange/yellow, orange-brown, purple, red, yellow, dark blue — corresponding to the 8 chunks visible in the global memory row below.

SECTION 2: GLOBAL MEMORY ROW

Labeled "Global Memory" on the left.
Contains 8 contiguous colored blocks, each representing an 8-byte (8B) chunk:
  Position 1: Green
  Position 2: Light Blue
  Position 3: Yellow/Orange
  Position 4: Orange-Brown
  Position 5: Purple
  Position 6: Red
  Position 7: Yellow
  Position 8: Dark Navy Blue

The label "LSB" appears above the left edge of the first (green) block, and "MSB" appears above the boundary between the first and second blocks, indicating bit ordering within each element.

A double-headed arrow labeled "8B" spans the width of one block (approximately the 3rd block, yellow/orange), indicating each colored block is 8 bytes wide.

The total global memory row is 8 blocks × 8B = 64 bytes of contiguous data.

Lines connect the left end (LSB) and right end of the .b4x16 bar at the top down to the left and right ends of the global memory row, showing the .b4x16 format maps to this 64-byte global memory region.

SECTION 3: TRANSFER OPERATION

A large downward-pointing blue arrow between the Global Memory row and the Shared Memory row is labeled "cp.async.bulk.tensor", indicating the asynchronous bulk tensor copy instruction performs this transformation.

SECTION 4: SHARED MEMORY ROW

Labeled "Shared Memory" on the left.
The shared memory row is wider than the global memory row, reflecting the padding insertion.

The row contains alternating colored blocks and white (empty/padding) blocks:
  Slot 1: Green block (8B data)
  Slot 2: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 3: Light Blue block (8B data)
  Slot 4: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 5: Yellow/Orange block (8B data)
  Slot 6: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 7: Orange-Brown block (8B data)
  Slot 8: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 9: Purple block (8B data)
  Slot 10: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 11: Red block (8B data)
  Slot 12: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 13: Yellow block (8B data)
  Slot 14: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 15: Dark Navy Blue block (8B data)
  Slot 16: White/empty block (8B padding)

Total slots: 16 (8 data + 8 padding)
Total shared memory size: 16 × 8B = 128 bytes

SECTION 5: DIMENSION ANNOTATIONS IN SHARED MEMORY

Two double-headed arrows are shown below the shared memory row in the region of the 3rd data block (yellow/orange) and its adjacent padding:
  First arrow labeled "8B": spans the width of one data block (8 bytes)
  Second arrow labeled "8B": spans the width of the adjacent padding block (8 bytes)

A larger double-headed arrow below these two is labeled ".b4x16_p64", spanning the combined width of one data block plus one padding block (8B + 8B = 16B total per unit). This defines the stride/period of the .b4x16_p64 layout: each 8-byte data element is followed by 8 bytes of padding, making each element's stride 16 bytes in shared memory.

SUMMARY OF LAYOUT PATTERN:

Format name: .b4x16_p64
Source (Global Memory): 64 bytes of contiguous data, organized as 8 chunks of 8 bytes each, packed with no gaps.
Destination (Shared Memory): 128 bytes total, organized as 8 data chunks of 8 bytes each, each followed by 8 bytes of padding.
Padding pattern: Every 8B of data is followed by 8B of zero/empty padding.
Stride per element in shared memory: 16 bytes (8B data + 8B pad).
The "p64" in the name refers to 64 bytes of padding total inserted across the entire layout (8 padding slots × 8B = 64B padding).
Data ordering is preserved: the color sequence (green, light blue, yellow, orange-brown, purple, red, yellow, dark blue) is identical between global and shared memory; only spacing changes.

LAYOUT FOR .b4x16_p64

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout transformation for the .b4x16_p64 format, showing how data is transferred from Global Memory to Shared Memory using the cp.async.bulk.tensor instruction. The layout involves a padding scheme where each 8-byte data chunk is followed by an 8-byte padding region in shared memory.

SECTION 1: SOURCE FORMAT (.b4x16 in Global Memory)

At the top of the figure, a wide horizontal bar is labeled ".b4x16" with "LSB" on the left end and "MSB" on the right end (with an arrow pointing right to MSB). This bar represents the .b4x16 format as a densely packed sequence of small colored cells (4-bit elements, 16 per group, repeating). The colors cycle through a repeating pattern: green, light blue, orange/yellow, orange-brown, purple, red, yellow, dark blue — corresponding to the 8 chunks visible in the global memory row below.

SECTION 2: GLOBAL MEMORY ROW

Labeled "Global Memory" on the left.
Contains 8 contiguous colored blocks, each representing an 8-byte (8B) chunk:
  Position 1: Green
  Position 2: Light Blue
  Position 3: Yellow/Orange
  Position 4: Orange-Brown
  Position 5: Purple
  Position 6: Red
  Position 7: Yellow
  Position 8: Dark Navy Blue

The label "LSB" appears above the left edge of the first (green) block, and "MSB" appears above the boundary between the first and second blocks, indicating bit ordering within each element.

A double-headed arrow labeled "8B" spans the width of one block (approximately the 3rd block, yellow/orange), indicating each colored block is 8 bytes wide.

The total global memory row is 8 blocks × 8B = 64 bytes of contiguous data.

Lines connect the left end (LSB) and right end of the .b4x16 bar at the top down to the left and right ends of the global memory row, showing the .b4x16 format maps to this 64-byte global memory region.

SECTION 3: TRANSFER OPERATION

A large downward-pointing blue arrow between the Global Memory row and the Shared Memory row is labeled "cp.async.bulk.tensor", indicating the asynchronous bulk tensor copy instruction performs this transformation.

SECTION 4: SHARED MEMORY ROW

Labeled "Shared Memory" on the left.
The shared memory row is wider than the global memory row, reflecting the padding insertion.

The row contains alternating colored blocks and white (empty/padding) blocks:
  Slot 1: Green block (8B data)
  Slot 2: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 3: Light Blue block (8B data)
  Slot 4: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 5: Yellow/Orange block (8B data)
  Slot 6: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 7: Orange-Brown block (8B data)
  Slot 8: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 9: Purple block (8B data)
  Slot 10: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 11: Red block (8B data)
  Slot 12: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 13: Yellow block (8B data)
  Slot 14: White/empty block (8B padding)
  Slot 15: Dark Navy Blue block (8B data)
  Slot 16: White/empty block (8B padding)

Total slots: 16 (8 data + 8 padding)
Total shared memory size: 16 × 8B = 128 bytes

SECTION 5: DIMENSION ANNOTATIONS IN SHARED MEMORY

Two double-headed arrows are shown below the shared memory row in the region of the 3rd data block (yellow/orange) and its adjacent padding:
  First arrow labeled "8B": spans the width of one data block (8 bytes)
  Second arrow labeled "8B": spans the width of the adjacent padding block (8 bytes)

A larger double-headed arrow below these two is labeled ".b4x16_p64", spanning the combined width of one data block plus one padding block (8B + 8B = 16B total per unit). This defines the stride/period of the .b4x16_p64 layout: each 8-byte data element is followed by 8 bytes of padding, making each element's stride 16 bytes in shared memory.

SUMMARY OF LAYOUT PATTERN:

Format name: .b4x16_p64
Source (Global Memory): 64 bytes of contiguous data, organized as 8 chunks of 8 bytes each, packed with no gaps.
Destination (Shared Memory): 128 bytes total, organized as 8 data chunks of 8 bytes each, each followed by 8 bytes of padding.
Padding pattern: Every 8B of data is followed by 8B of zero/empty padding.
Stride per element in shared memory: 16 bytes (8B data + 8B pad).
The "p64" in the name refers to 64 bytes of padding total inserted across the entire layout (8 padding slots × 8B = 64B padding).
Data ordering is preserved: the color sequence (green, light blue, yellow, orange-brown, purple, red, yellow, dark blue) is identical between global and shared memory; only spacing changes.


   Figure 5 Layout for .b4x16_p64

   The padded region that gets added is un-initialized.
3. Type `.b6x16_p32`:
   With this type, sixteen 6-bits of data is copied from global memory to the shared memory
   with an append of 32-bits of padding as shown in
   [Figure 6](#tensor-dimension-size-format-sub-bytes-padding-align-b6-16-p32)

   



[Figure: tensor-dimension-size-format-sub-bytes-padding-align-b6-16-p32]
LAYOUT DESCRIPTION FOR .b6x16_p32

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout transformation for the .b6x16_p32 format, showing how data is transferred from Global Memory to Shared Memory using the cp.async.bulk.tensor instruction. The layout involves 6-bit elements packed into a 16-element vector, with padding added in shared memory.

SECTION 1: .b6x16 SOURCE FORMAT (Top of Figure)
- A wide horizontal strip labeled ".b6x16" at the top
- Labeled with "LSB" at the left end and "MSB" further right (indicating bit ordering within elements)
- The strip contains many small colored cells arranged in a repeating color pattern
- Colors cycle through: green, blue/light blue, orange/yellow, orange-brown, purple, red, yellow, dark navy blue
- This represents the packed 6-bit x 16 element format in its native form
- The strip spans the full width of the figure, indicating a large number of elements
- Two arrows point from the left end and right end of this strip down to the Global Memory row, indicating the mapping relationship

SECTION 2: GLOBAL MEMORY LAYOUT (Middle of Figure)
- Labeled "Global Memory" on the left
- "LSB" and "MSB" labels appear above the left portion of the row
- Contains 8 colored blocks arranged sequentially (left to right):
  1. Green block
  2. Light blue block
  3. Orange/yellow block (larger)
  4. Orange-brown block (larger)
  5. Purple block (larger)
  6. Red block (larger)
  7. Yellow block (larger)
  8. Dark navy blue block (larger)
- A double-headed arrow beneath the orange/yellow block indicates a width of 12B (12 bytes)
- The 12B measurement spans one element's storage size in global memory
- Each group of 16 six-bit elements (6 bits x 16 = 96 bits = 12 bytes) occupies 12B in global memory
- The two diagonal lines from the .b6x16 strip above connect to the left and right ends of this global memory row

SECTION 3: TRANSFER OPERATION
- A large blue downward arrow in the center
- Labeled "cp.async.bulk.tensor" to the right of the arrow
- This indicates the asynchronous bulk tensor copy instruction performs the transformation

SECTION 4: SHARED MEMORY LAYOUT (Bottom of Figure)
- Labeled "Shared Memory" on the left
- Contains the same 8 colored blocks as global memory, but now each colored block is followed by a white/empty padding block:
  1. Green block + white padding
  2. Light blue block + white padding
  3. Orange/yellow block + white padding
  4. Orange-brown block + white padding
  5. Purple block + white padding
  6. Red block + white padding
  7. Yellow block + white padding
  8. Dark navy blue block + white padding
- Two measurement annotations appear below this row:
  - "12B" with a double-headed arrow spanning the colored data portion of one element
  - "4B" with a double-headed arrow spanning the white padding portion immediately after
  - Together these are labeled ".b6x16_p32" with a combined double-headed arrow spanning both the 12B data and 4B padding
  - Total per-element stride in shared memory = 12B + 4B = 16B (128 bits = 16 bytes per element group)

KEY RELATIONSHIPS AND CALCULATIONS:
- Source element size: 6 bits per element x 16 elements = 96 bits = 12 bytes (packed, no padding)
- In global memory: elements are stored packed at 12B per 16-element group
- In shared memory (.b6x16_p32): each 12B data block is followed by 4B of padding
- The "_p32" suffix indicates 32-bit (4-byte) padding appended after each 12B data chunk
- Total shared memory stride per 16-element group = 12B + 4B = 16B
- The padding aligns each 16-element group to a 16-byte (128-bit) boundary in shared memory
- Color coding is consistent between global and shared memory, confirming data values are preserved; only layout changes with the addition of padding
- The figure shows 8 such 16-element groups (8 colors), demonstrating the repeating pattern across a larger tensor

LAYOUT DESCRIPTION FOR .b6x16_p32

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout transformation for the .b6x16_p32 format, showing how data is transferred from Global Memory to Shared Memory using the cp.async.bulk.tensor instruction. The layout involves 6-bit elements packed into a 16-element vector, with padding added in shared memory.

SECTION 1: .b6x16 SOURCE FORMAT (Top of Figure)
- A wide horizontal strip labeled ".b6x16" at the top
- Labeled with "LSB" at the left end and "MSB" further right (indicating bit ordering within elements)
- The strip contains many small colored cells arranged in a repeating color pattern
- Colors cycle through: green, blue/light blue, orange/yellow, orange-brown, purple, red, yellow, dark navy blue
- This represents the packed 6-bit x 16 element format in its native form
- The strip spans the full width of the figure, indicating a large number of elements
- Two arrows point from the left end and right end of this strip down to the Global Memory row, indicating the mapping relationship

SECTION 2: GLOBAL MEMORY LAYOUT (Middle of Figure)
- Labeled "Global Memory" on the left
- "LSB" and "MSB" labels appear above the left portion of the row
- Contains 8 colored blocks arranged sequentially (left to right):
  1. Green block
  2. Light blue block
  3. Orange/yellow block (larger)
  4. Orange-brown block (larger)
  5. Purple block (larger)
  6. Red block (larger)
  7. Yellow block (larger)
  8. Dark navy blue block (larger)
- A double-headed arrow beneath the orange/yellow block indicates a width of 12B (12 bytes)
- The 12B measurement spans one element's storage size in global memory
- Each group of 16 six-bit elements (6 bits x 16 = 96 bits = 12 bytes) occupies 12B in global memory
- The two diagonal lines from the .b6x16 strip above connect to the left and right ends of this global memory row

SECTION 3: TRANSFER OPERATION
- A large blue downward arrow in the center
- Labeled "cp.async.bulk.tensor" to the right of the arrow
- This indicates the asynchronous bulk tensor copy instruction performs the transformation

SECTION 4: SHARED MEMORY LAYOUT (Bottom of Figure)
- Labeled "Shared Memory" on the left
- Contains the same 8 colored blocks as global memory, but now each colored block is followed by a white/empty padding block:
  1. Green block + white padding
  2. Light blue block + white padding
  3. Orange/yellow block + white padding
  4. Orange-brown block + white padding
  5. Purple block + white padding
  6. Red block + white padding
  7. Yellow block + white padding
  8. Dark navy blue block + white padding
- Two measurement annotations appear below this row:
  - "12B" with a double-headed arrow spanning the colored data portion of one element
  - "4B" with a double-headed arrow spanning the white padding portion immediately after
  - Together these are labeled ".b6x16_p32" with a combined double-headed arrow spanning both the 12B data and 4B padding
  - Total per-element stride in shared memory = 12B + 4B = 16B (128 bits = 16 bytes per element group)

KEY RELATIONSHIPS AND CALCULATIONS:
- Source element size: 6 bits per element x 16 elements = 96 bits = 12 bytes (packed, no padding)
- In global memory: elements are stored packed at 12B per 16-element group
- In shared memory (.b6x16_p32): each 12B data block is followed by 4B of padding
- The "_p32" suffix indicates 32-bit (4-byte) padding appended after each 12B data chunk
- Total shared memory stride per 16-element group = 12B + 4B = 16B
- The padding aligns each 16-element group to a 16-byte (128-bit) boundary in shared memory
- Color coding is consistent between global and shared memory, confirming data values are preserved; only layout changes with the addition of padding
- The figure shows 8 such 16-element groups (8 colors), demonstrating the repeating pattern across a larger tensor


   Figure 6 Layout for .b6x16_p32

   The padded region that gets added is un-initialized.
4. Type `.b6p2x16`:
   With this type, sixteen elements, each containing 6-bits of data at the LSB and 2-bits
   of padding at the MSB, are copied from shared memory into the global memory by discarding
   the 2-bits of padding data and packing the 6-bits data contiguously as shown in
   [Figure 7](#tensor-dimension-size-format-sub-bytes-padding-align-b6-p2-16)

   



[Figure: tensor-dimension-size-format-sub-bytes-padding-align-b6-p2-16]
LAYOUT FOR .b6p2x16

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout and data transfer pattern for the .b6p2x16 format in NVIDIA PTX ISA. It shows how data is organized in Shared Memory and how it maps to Global Memory via a cp.async.bulk.tensor operation.

STRUCTURE:
The figure contains two horizontal memory strips (rows of small cells/boxes) connected by a downward-pointing blue arrow labeled "cp.async.bulk.tensor".

TOP STRIP: Shared Memory
- Labeled "Shared Memory" on the left
- Has "LSB" and "MSB" labels at the top-left, indicating bit ordering within elements (LSB on the left, MSB to its right)
- The strip is wider/longer than the Global Memory strip, indicating it contains more total cells
- The cells are arranged in a single horizontal row
- Cells are color-coded in a repeating pattern to indicate grouping of elements
- Color groups visible (left to right): groups of light green/white cells, yellow cells, light blue/periwinkle cells, yellow/tan cells, salmon/orange cells, light green cells, blue cells, yellow cells, orange/red cells — this pattern repeats across the full width
- The Shared Memory strip appears to contain padding bits (the .b6p2 format stores 6-bit data with 2 padding bits per element, packed into a wider container)
- Total width appears to represent 16 elements (x16 in the format name) with padding included

BOTTOM STRIP: Global Memory
- Labeled "Global Memory" on the left
- Shorter/narrower than the Shared Memory strip, indicating fewer total bits stored
- Same color-coded grouping pattern as Shared Memory but without the padding bits
- Represents the packed 6-bit data without the 2 padding bits per element
- The color groups correspond to the same logical elements as in Shared Memory

ARROW / OPERATION:
- A large solid blue downward-pointing arrow connects Shared Memory (top) to Global Memory (bottom)
- The arrow is labeled "cp.async.bulk.tensor"
- This indicates the operation strips the padding bits (the 2 padding bits per element in .b6p2) when transferring from Shared Memory to Global Memory, compacting the 6-bit data

FORMAT INTERPRETATION (.b6p2x16):
- .b6p2: Each element is 6 bits of data with 2 bits of padding (total 8 bits = 1 byte per element in Shared Memory)
- x16: 16 elements per tile/vector
- In Shared Memory: 16 elements x 8 bits = 128 bits total (16 bytes), with LSB-first ordering
- In Global Memory: 16 elements x 6 bits = 96 bits (12 bytes), packed without padding
- The cp.async.bulk.tensor operation handles the format conversion, removing the 2 padding bits from each element during the store to Global Memory

COLOR CODING:
- Each distinct color group represents one logical 6-bit data element
- The same color appears in both Shared Memory and Global Memory strips, showing the correspondence between padded (Shared) and packed (Global) representations
- Approximately 16 distinct color groups are visible, consistent with the x16 designation

BIT ORDERING:
- LSB is at the left end of each element in Shared Memory
- MSB is to the right of LSB within each element
- This is explicitly marked at the top-left of the Shared Memory strip

LAYOUT FOR .b6p2x16

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the memory layout and data transfer pattern for the .b6p2x16 format in NVIDIA PTX ISA. It shows how data is organized in Shared Memory and how it maps to Global Memory via a cp.async.bulk.tensor operation.

STRUCTURE:
The figure contains two horizontal memory strips (rows of small cells/boxes) connected by a downward-pointing blue arrow labeled "cp.async.bulk.tensor".

TOP STRIP: Shared Memory
- Labeled "Shared Memory" on the left
- Has "LSB" and "MSB" labels at the top-left, indicating bit ordering within elements (LSB on the left, MSB to its right)
- The strip is wider/longer than the Global Memory strip, indicating it contains more total cells
- The cells are arranged in a single horizontal row
- Cells are color-coded in a repeating pattern to indicate grouping of elements
- Color groups visible (left to right): groups of light green/white cells, yellow cells, light blue/periwinkle cells, yellow/tan cells, salmon/orange cells, light green cells, blue cells, yellow cells, orange/red cells — this pattern repeats across the full width
- The Shared Memory strip appears to contain padding bits (the .b6p2 format stores 6-bit data with 2 padding bits per element, packed into a wider container)
- Total width appears to represent 16 elements (x16 in the format name) with padding included

BOTTOM STRIP: Global Memory
- Labeled "Global Memory" on the left
- Shorter/narrower than the Shared Memory strip, indicating fewer total bits stored
- Same color-coded grouping pattern as Shared Memory but without the padding bits
- Represents the packed 6-bit data without the 2 padding bits per element
- The color groups correspond to the same logical elements as in Shared Memory

ARROW / OPERATION:
- A large solid blue downward-pointing arrow connects Shared Memory (top) to Global Memory (bottom)
- The arrow is labeled "cp.async.bulk.tensor"
- This indicates the operation strips the padding bits (the 2 padding bits per element in .b6p2) when transferring from Shared Memory to Global Memory, compacting the 6-bit data

FORMAT INTERPRETATION (.b6p2x16):
- .b6p2: Each element is 6 bits of data with 2 bits of padding (total 8 bits = 1 byte per element in Shared Memory)
- x16: 16 elements per tile/vector
- In Shared Memory: 16 elements x 8 bits = 128 bits total (16 bytes), with LSB-first ordering
- In Global Memory: 16 elements x 6 bits = 96 bits (12 bytes), packed without padding
- The cp.async.bulk.tensor operation handles the format conversion, removing the 2 padding bits from each element during the store to Global Memory

COLOR CODING:
- Each distinct color group represents one logical 6-bit data element
- The same color appears in both Shared Memory and Global Memory strips, showing the correspondence between padded (Shared) and packed (Global) representations
- Approximately 16 distinct color groups are visible, consistent with the x16 designation

BIT ORDERING:
- LSB is at the left end of each element in Shared Memory
- MSB is to the right of LSB within each element
- This is explicitly marked at the top-left of the Shared Memory strip


   Figure 7 Layout for .b6p2x16

In case of `.b6x16_p32` and `.b4x16_p64`, the padded region that gets added is
un-initialized.

The types `.b6x16_p32` and `.b6p2x16` share the same encoding value in the
descriptor (value 15) as the two types are applicable for different types of
tensor copy operations:

| Type | Valid Tensor Copy Direction |
| --- | --- |
| `.b6x16_p32` | `.shared::cluster.global`, `.shared::cta.global` |
| `.b6p2x16` | `.global.shared::cta` |
